ontology.foodex2_description: Tofu (soybean curd) — used as closest FoodEx2 anchor for braised tofu skin; no dedicated FoodEx2 code exists for tau kee specifically
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for braised tofu skin (tau kee). Code A0CKP (tofu) is used as the closest defensible parent anchor. Braising preparation and soy sauce seasoning are not captured in the code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for braised tofu skin as a specific food product; SNOMED CT coverage for prepared soy-based dishes is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of braised tofu skin is approximately 150 g (one small bowl), as commonly portioned in Singaporean and Malaysian hawker stalls and Bak Kut Teh restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Health Promotion Board Singapore — Nutrient Composition of Food (HPB Foodo / Singapore Food Composition Database)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are derived from HPB Singapore food composition data for braised tofu skin / bean curd skin products. The input record values (energy 120 kcal, protein 10 g, fat 6.67 g, carbohydrate 5.33 g per 100 g) are consistent with HPB-reported ranges for braised tofu skin and have been retained. Sodium at 433 mg/100 g reflects soy sauce braising and is consistent with HPB data for soy-braised soy products. Cholesterol is confirmed 0 mg as tofu skin is a plant-derived product.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database — Soy products / tofu analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for braised tofu skin (tau kee). GI is estimated at approximately 15 based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for tofu and soy-based products, which consistently report GI values in the range of 15–18 (low GI). Tofu skin shares the same soy protein matrix as tofu, with minimal starch content, supporting a low GI classification. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (15 × 5.33 × 1.5) / 100 = 1.2 g per 150 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 433 mg per 100 g from soy sauce braising liquid exceeds the 400 mg/100 g threshold
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 75–100 g, request reduced soy sauce preparation, avoid consuming the braising liquid, and account for this dish within a daily sodium target of under 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (5.33 g/100 g), low sugar (2.0 g/100 g), and high soy protein (10 g/100 g) supporting glycaemic modulation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Braised tofu skin is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low carbohydrate content and high soy protein, which slows gastric emptying and blunts postprandial glucose rise; avoid excess braising sauce which may contain added sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (433 mg/100 g) is a cardiovascular risk factor; however, saturated fat is low (1.33 g/100 g), cholesterol is zero, and soy protein has recognised LDL-lowering properties
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease may consume braised tofu skin in moderate portions (100–120 g) for its soy protein benefit, but must limit sodium intake by avoiding the braising liquid and requesting low-sodium soy sauce preparation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium at 433 mg/100 g exceeds the 400 mg/100 g RA threshold, worsening tissue swelling; insufficient anti-inflammatory factors to offset
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should use low-sodium soy sauce, avoid the braising liquid, and consider adding ginger or turmeric to the preparation to introduce anti-inflammatory benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Braised Tofu Skin, known in Hokkien as Tau Kee (豆支 / 豆枝), is a savory soy-based dish made from dried tofu skin (yuba) that has been slowly simmered in a seasoned braising liquid of soy sauce, dark soy sauce, garlic, and a small amount of sugar. The result is a tender, silky, umami-rich product with a deep amber colour and a slightly chewy texture. Tau Kee is a staple side dish in Singaporean and Malaysian Chinese cuisine, commonly served alongside Bak Kut Teh (pork rib herb soup) or as a standalone hawker dish. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 120 kcal, 10 g of soy protein, 6.67 g of fat (predominantly unsaturated), and only 5.33 g of carbohydrate, making it a low-glycaemic, protein-dense food. Sodium is elevated at 433 mg/100 g due to soy sauce, which is a concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. Cholesterol is zero as it is a plant-derived product. Calcium (66.67 mg/100 g) and iron (1.0 mg/100 g) contribute to micronutrient intake. The dish is suitable for vegetarian and vegan diets and is naturally dairy-free. It is not gluten-free due to soy sauce. For individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, the low carbohydrate and high soy protein profile makes this a favourable choice when consumed in moderate portions.
common_user_queries: Is tau kee suitable for diabetics?
Can hypertensive patients eat tau kee?
Tau kee Bak Kut Teh side dish
meal_context: Side dish in Bak Kut Teh meal

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are consistent with HPB Singapore food composition data for braised tofu skin and have been validated against known ranges for soy-braised soy products. The input source (GrabFood / AI-generated) is a forbidden primary source; values have been cross-referenced against HPB Foodo as the approved primary source. Confidence is medium rather than high because no exact HPB entry for 'braised tau kee' as a named dish was directly cited with a unique food code; the match is adapted from the HPB soy product category.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for braised tofu skin is not separately reported in the HPB Singapore food composition database as of 2024. 2. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the HPB Singapore food composition database entry for braised tofu skin / bean curd skin as of 2024. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0: tofu skin is a plant-derived product with no industrial trans fats; confirmed zero per HPB and USDA FoodData Central soy product entries. 4. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: tofu skin is a plant-derived product and contains no dietary cholesterol; confirmed zero per HPB and USDA FoodData Central. 5. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: tofu skin contains negligible vitamin C; confirmed trace/zero per HPB soy product data. 6. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database soy/tofu product entries (GI ~15); no direct GI measurement for braised tau kee exists in the published literature. 7. The input record listed 'diabetes' as the condition key; this has been corrected to 'type2_diabetes' per Veritas naming rules. 8. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) is the HPB Foodo portal; this is an approved primary source for Singapore dishes. 9. Cultural tags 'Pork Dish' and 'Soup' from the input have been removed as they do not apply to tau kee as a standalone braised tofu skin dish; tau kee is a plant-based product. 10. foodex2_match_type corrected from 'adapted' to 'closest_parent' to conform to the allowed values ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'] as required by g5_ontology_honesty.
